question: Which statement about signal cardinality and performance in Camunda 8.8 is FALSE?

options.text: A single broadcasted signal triggers all signal catch events whose signal name matches.
A signal broadcast reaches all partitions of the cluster.
Signals scale better than messages because each broadcast targets a single partition selected by a correlation key.
Network traffic from a signal broadcast scales linearly with the number of partitions.

a.text: True per the docs — a broadcasted signal iterates over all available subscriptions and triggers every catch event whose signal name matches.

b.text: True per the docs — signals are broadcast to all partitions.

c.text: False — signals have no correlation key; they are broadcast to all partitions and to all matching subscriptions, which is exactly why the docs caution that signals can negatively impact Camunda 8 performance compared to correlated messages.

d.text: True per the docs — the caution notes that broadcast network traffic scales linearly with the number of partitions.

explanation: The Signal cardinality section warns that a single signal broadcast iterates over all matching subscriptions and is sent to all partitions, with network traffic growing linearly in the number of partitions. Unlike messages, signals do not use a correlation key, so they cannot be routed to a single partition. That is precisely why the docs caution that signals can negatively impact Camunda 8 performance.
